Step 1: Recall that the cystic artery supplies the gallbladder and almost always arises from the right hepatic artery.  
Step 2: The right hepatic artery itself is a branch of the proper hepatic artery (which comes off the common hepatic).  
Step 3: Therefore, to ligate the cystic artery safely you trace it back to its origin at the right hepatic artery.  

Final Answer: \boxed{E}

Step 1 (Clinical scenario): Six weeks ago the patient suffered ischemic acute tubular necrosis (ATN) in the setting of cardiac arrest. One week later (5 weeks ago) he was oliguric with elevated BUN and creatinine and muddy-brown (granular) casts, consistent with the maintenance phase of ATN.

Step 2 (Recovery): Four weeks ago (i.e. 2 weeks into the course of ATN) his oliguria resolved and his BUN/creatinine returned to normal. This indicates he has entered the recovery phase of ATN.

Step 3 (Pathology of recovery from ATN): In ischemic ATN the tubular basement membranes generally remain intact. Surviving tubular epithelial cells proliferate and thereby restore the normal epithelial lining. Over weeks, the kidneys return to essentially normal histologic appearance.

Step 4 (Autopsy at 6 weeks): By six weeks after an episode of ATN that has clinically resolved, the kidney architecture is expected to be normal.

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Normal kidney (E)}}

Step 1: Identify the study design  
- This is a retrospective case–control study (cases with mesothelioma vs. controls without).  

Step 2: Note the key feature  
- All subjects (both cases and controls) must have had chest x-rays.  

Step 3: Consider why you’d require chest x-rays in controls  
- If controls didn’t have x-rays, some might actually have undiagnosed mesothelioma.  
- Ensuring both groups had the same diagnostic opportunity prevents differences in disease detection.  

Step 4: Match this rationale to the answer choices  
(A) Confounding – unrelated to requiring x-rays  
(B) Demonstrate causality – cohort or trial design, not x-ray requirement  
(C) Minimize ascertainment bias – yes: equalizes detection/surveillance in cases and controls  
(D) Reduce recall bias – data come from records, not patient memory  

Final answer: \boxed{C}

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– The patient is an elderly man with 4 weeks of symmetric shoulder/upper‐arm stiffness, worse in the morning and improving later in the day.  
– He has systemic “flu‐like” symptoms (fatigue, low‐grade fever, headache) and a markedly elevated ESR.  
– Normal CK argues against an inflammatory myositis.  
– Shoulders have normal range of motion without true joint inflammation.  

These findings are classic for polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR).  

Step 2: Recognize risk factors for PMR  
– PMR almost exclusively occurs in patients over age 50 (peak in the 70s).  
– It is more common in women than men.  
– There is no strong association with osteoarthritis, statin use, or specific occupations.  

Step 3: Choose the most significant risk factor in this patient  
Among the options:  
(A) Age – PMR occurs almost only in patients >50, with incidence rising with age.  
(B) Gender – although women are at higher risk than men, gender is less potent than age.  
(C) History of osteoarthritis – no known link to PMR.  
(D) Previous occupation – no known link to PMR.  
(E) Use of pravastatin – statins can cause myalgias but not PMR.  

The single most important risk factor for PMR is advanced age.  

Final answer: \boxed{Age (A)}

Step 1: Recognize the MRI finding.  
A “central syrinx” in the cervical cord is syringomyelia, which classically causes a “cape-like” loss of pain and temperature and weakness in the upper extremities.

Step 2: Recall common causes of syringomyelia.  
• Chiari I malformation (congenital herniation of cerebellar tonsils)  
• Trauma – post‐traumatic cavitation of the cord  
• Neoplasm, infection, or inflammation (less common in this scenario)

Step 3: Use the clinical clues.  
• The patient is 20 (age fits Chiari but also could have a remote history of trauma)  
• She has headache and back pain (could be from Chiari, but trauma is also a key precipitant)  
• No congenital diagnosis is mentioned

Step 4: Which history would be most helpful?  
Asking about prior head or spinal cord trauma can uncover a post-traumatic syringomyelia.

Answer: It is most appropriate to obtain additional history regarding trauma.  
Final answer: \boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify key clinical features  
– Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, dry skin, brittle hair, ankle edema, lethargy  
– Bradycardia (55/min), mild hypertension (150/90)  
– Prolonged relaxation phase of deep tendon reflexes  
→ All consistent with hypothyroidism  

Step 2: Examine the thyroid gland  
– Nontender, diffusely enlarged (≈2× normal) goiter  

Step 3: Match to most likely cause of primary hypothyroidism with a painless goiter in an older woman  
– Hashimoto (chronic lymphocytic) thyroiditis: most common cause of hypothyroidism in adults; painless diffuse goiter  
– Other choices:  
   • Defect in T4 biosynthesis (dyshormonogenesis) – usually presents in childhood  
   • Graves disease – hyperthyroidism  
   • Multinodular goiter – usually euthyroid or hyper, nodular rather than diffuse  
   • Riedel thyroiditis – “woody,” hard thyroid, rare  
   • Thyroid cyst/lymphoma – focal lesions, often rapid enlargement or pain  

Final Answer: \boxed{A} chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis (Hashimoto disease)

Step 1: Recall the Starling equation for glomerular filtration rate (GFR):  
GFR = Kf × [(P_GC – P_BS) – (π_GC – π_BS)]  
where  
– P_GC = glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure  
– P_BS = Bowman’s space hydrostatic pressure  
– π_GC = glomerular capillary oncotic pressure  
– π_BS = Bowman’s space oncotic pressure (normally ≈0)

Step 2: Understand what happens when you constrict the efferent arteriole (downstream resistance ↑) while keeping afferent pressure constant.  
– Downstream resistance ↑ → blood “backs up” in glomerular capillaries → P_GC rises  
– Renal blood flow (and RPF) falls → filtration fraction ↑ → protein concentration in capillaries rises along their length → π_GC rises modestly  
– Kf (filtration coefficient) is unchanged (it depends on surface area and permeability, not acute resistance changes)  
– P_BS and π_BS remain essentially unchanged

Step 3: Identify which Starling force changes most prominently.  
The predominant immediate change is an ↑ in glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure (P_GC), which increases net filtration pressure and thus GFR (up to the point where the oncotic rise counter-balances it).

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Increased hydrostatic pressure}} (Choice D)

Step 1: Identify the type of acute kidney injury  
– BUN/Cr ratio is about 73/4.2 ≃ 17, not strongly prerenal  
– Ultrasound shows a distended bladder and bilateral hydronephrosis → postrenal (obstructive) AKI  

Step 2: Note prior attempts to relieve obstruction  
– Several failed urethral catheterizations in the nursing facility  
– Current attempts at urethral catheterization have also failed  

Step 3: Consider options for relieving bladder outlet obstruction  
– Bethanechol: stimulates detrusor contraction but is contraindicated if there is a mechanical obstruction  
– Prazosin: an α₁-blocker used for BPH but patient’s prostate is normal in size and no evidence of BPH  
– Hemodialysis: indicated for refractory hyperkalemia, volume overload, or uremic complications, but here the primary problem is obstruction that can be relieved  
– Ultrafiltration: for diuretic‐refractory volume overload, not first‐line for postrenal obstruction  
– Suprapubic catheter: bypasses urethra and directly decompresses the bladder  

Step 4: Choose the intervention that relieves the obstruction  
The most appropriate next step is to place a suprapubic catheter to decompress the bladder and relieve the postrenal obstruction.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Placement of a suprapubic catheter}}

Step 1: Identify the key findings  
– 9-year-old with a harsh systolic murmur along the left sternal border  
– Weak femoral pulses with radiofemoral delay  
– CXR: mild cardiomegaly with left ventricular prominence  
– ECG: left ventricular hypertrophy  

These findings—especially the radiofemoral delay and LVH—are characteristic of coarctation of the aorta.

Step 2: Recall complications of aortic coarctation  
– Upper-body (proximal) hypertension  
– Left ventricular hypertrophy and eventual heart failure  
– Berry aneurysms in the circle of Willis → risk of intracranial hemorrhage  
– Increased risk for infective endocarditis  

Step 3: Match to the answer choices  
A. Atrial fibrillation – not a classic complication  
B. Cor pulmonale – right-heart problem, not seen here  
C. Systemic hypertension – yes; patients develop persistent proximal HTN  
D. Tricuspid regurgitation – not associated  

Final Answer: \boxed{C}

Step 1: Identify his risk factors and current problems  
- He is morbidly obese (BMI 38) and has a family history of diabetes, hypertension, and CAD.  
- Despite 3 months of lifestyle modification (walking and salt restriction), his blood pressure remains elevated at 156/98 mm Hg.  
- His fasting glucose is 104 mg/dL (impaired fasting glucose), LDL is 137 mg/dL (borderline high), HDL is low at 33 mg/dL, and triglycerides are 158 mg/dL.

Step 2: Determine which finding most urgently requires pharmacotherapy  
- He has stage 1 hypertension (BP ≥ 140/90) persisting despite lifestyle changes—guidelines recommend starting drug therapy once lifestyle measures alone are insufficient.  
- His prediabetes (fasting glucose 100–125) could be managed by continued lifestyle changes; pharmacologic prevention with metformin is used selectively (e.g., BMI > 35 in younger patients), but his more pressing issue is uncontrolled hypertension.  
- His LDL of 137 mg/dL in a 28-year-old without diabetes or established atherosclerotic disease does not yet meet criteria for primary‐prevention statin therapy (10-year ASCVD risk is likely low).  
- He is tachycardic (pulse 102), but reducing heart rate–alone (eg, with a β-blocker) is not the first priority absent another indication.

Step 3: Choose the primary target for pharmacotherapy  
Given persistent stage 1 hypertension despite lifestyle modification, the next step is to add an antihypertensive agent to lower his blood pressure.

Final Answer:  
Pharmacotherapy should be directed toward lowering blood pressure.  
\boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify key findings  
- Remote gunshot wound to the thigh (5 years ago)  
- Current “buzzing” sensation at the scar site  
- Loud murmur (bruit) on auscultation and palpable thrill  
- Dilated varicose veins with incompetent valves in the lower leg  

Step 2: Interpret the findings  
- A bruit and thrill over a wound site suggest turbulent, high‐flow communication between an artery and vein.  
- Chronic arteriovenous shunting raises venous pressure, leading to venous dilation and valve incompetence (varicose veins).

Step 3: Consider the answer choices  
(A) Arterial spasm – would cause ischemia signs, not a bruit/thrill or varicosities  
(B) Arteriovenous fistula – explains a continuous murmur/bruit, thrill, and chronic venous hypertension  
(C) Deep venous thrombosis – causes swelling and pain, not a bruit or thrill  
(D) Occlusion of the superficial femoral artery – causes diminished pulses and limb ischemia, not a thrill/bruit  
(E) Pseudoaneurysm – can produce a bruit, but does not typically cause chronic venous hypertension and varicosities  

Step 4: Select the most likely diagnosis  
- Chronic high‐flow shunt with bruit, thrill, and venous dilation = arteriovenous fistula

Final Answer  
\boxed{(B)\text{ Arteriovenous fistula}}

Step 1: Identify the problem  
– The patient has recurrent calcium kidney stones.  
– A 24-hour urine collection shows increased urinary calcium (hypercalciuria).

Step 2: Determine the desired pharmacologic effect  
– We want to lower urinary calcium excretion to reduce stone formation.

Step 3: Review diuretics’ effects on calcium handling  
– Loop diuretics (Na⁺–K⁺–2Cl⁻ symport inhibitors) increase urinary Ca²⁺ (bad for stones).  
– Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors also increase urinary Ca²⁺.  
– Osmotic diuretics increase overall urine flow and Ca²⁺ excretion.  
– ENaC inhibitors (amiloride) have minimal effect on Ca²⁺ excretion.  
– Thiazide diuretics (Na⁺–Cl⁻ symport inhibitors) *decrease* urinary Ca²⁺ by enhancing Ca²⁺ reabsorption in the distal tubule.

Step 4: Choose the best agent  
– A thiazide diuretic is the most appropriate to treat hypercalciuria and prevent calcium stones.

Final Answer: \boxed{B} (Na⁺–Cl⁻ symport inhibitor, e.g., hydrochlorothiazide)

Step 1: Key features  
– 8-year-old with fever (39.4°C), sore throat, itchy eyes  
– Exam: conjunctival injection with discharge + oropharyngeal erythema  
– History of recent exposure: swimming pool, outbreak among campers  

Step 2: Differential diagnosis  
– Adenovirus: causes “pharyngoconjunctival fever,” often in children at swimming pools; presents with fever, sore throat, conjunctivitis, sometimes diarrhea  
– CMV and EBV: can cause pharyngitis but not typically conjunctivitis or swimming-pool outbreaks  
– Influenza: causes fever and sore throat but not usually prominent conjunctivitis in clusters at pools  
– West Nile virus: transmitted by mosquitoes, presents with fever, sometimes rash or neuroinvasive disease, not pharyngoconjunctival syndrome  

Step 3: Most likely cause  
Pharyngoconjunctival fever in a pool-associated outbreak is classic for adenovirus infection.

Final Answer: \boxed{A\;\text{(Adenovirus)}}

Step 1: Clinical picture  
– 45-year-old with epigastric burning, night pain, postprandial pain, weight loss, positive fecal occult blood → peptic ulcer disease.  
– Endoscopy: 3 cm bleeding ulcer in the gastric antrum.  

Step 2: Histology  
– Steiner silver stain at 400× shows curved/spiral organisms along the mucosal surface.  
– This is characteristic of Helicobacter pylori.  

Step 3: H. pylori pathogenesis  
– Colonizes antrum of the stomach and produces large amounts of urease (to neutralize gastric acid by producing ammonia)  
– Secretes proteases and toxins that injure the mucosal barrier and induce inflammation  
– Results in mucosal damage and ulcer formation  

Answer choice A (“Elaboration of proteases and urease with local tissue destruction”) precisely describes H. pylori’s virulence mechanisms.  

Final answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: A positive urine “reducing substances” test with a negative glucose oxidase test tells us there is a reducing sugar other than glucose in the urine (e.g. galactose or fructose).  
Step 2: This infant is 6 days old and exclusively breast-fed, so the sugar in question must come from lactose (i.e. glucose + galactose), not dietary fructose.  
Step 3: Classic presentation—jaundice, hepatomegaly, vomiting—shortly after initiating milk feeds, with galactose in the urine, is due to galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase deficiency (classic galactosemia).  
Step 4: In this condition, galactose-1-phosphate builds up in hepatocytes.  

Therefore, the metabolite most likely elevated in his liver is  
\boxed{Galactose\;1\text{-}phosphate}   (Answer choice B)

Step 1: Identify the key findings  
– Persistent hypertension (home readings >150/90 and office 152/98 mm Hg)  
– Mild fatigue, trace ankle edema  
– Lab evidence of hypokalemia (K⁺ 3.1 mEq/L) with normal renal function  

Step 2: Generate causes of secondary hypertension with hypokalemia  
– Primary hyperaldosteronism (“Conn syndrome”): autonomous aldosterone secretion → Na⁺ retention, K⁺ wasting → hypertension + hypokalemia  
– Cushing syndrome: cortisol excess can mineralocorticoid-like effects → hypokalemia, but patient lacks Cushing stigmata (central obesity, striae, moon facies)  
– Renovascular hypertension (e.g., fibromuscular dysplasia): typically elevated renin and secondary aldosterone; hypokalemia is less prominent unless severe; bruits often heard  
– Obstructive sleep apnea: common cause of resistant hypertension, but does not characteristically cause hypokalemia  
– Hypothyroidism: can cause diastolic hypertension, but not hypokalemia  

Step 3: Match this patient’s presentation  
– Hypertension + unexplained hypokalemia in the absence of diuretic use strongly suggests primary hyperaldosteronism.  

Final Answer: \boxed{Hyperaldosteronism} (Choice C)

Step 1: Identify the clinical problem  
– A hospital‐acquired infectious diarrhea (most likely C. difficile) leading to profuse watery stools, abdominal cramping, and signs of volume depletion (dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, borderline hypotension).  

Step 2: Assess laboratory evidence of volume depletion  
– BUN/Cr = 30/1.5 = 20 (suggests prerenal azotemia)  
– Hypernatremia (Na⁺ 150 mEq/L) from free water losses  
– Mild metabolic acidosis (HCO₃⁻ 22) from bicarbonate loss in the stool  

Step 3: Determine immediate management priorities  
– Restore intravascular volume to correct hypotension and prerenal azotemia  
– Replace free water deficit more slowly to correct hypernatremia once perfusion is restored  

Step 4: Choose the appropriate intravenous fluid  
– 0.9% saline (isotonic) is the fluid of choice for initial volume resuscitation in hypovolemic patients  
– D5W would not adequately expand the intravascular compartment and is better for free-water replacement only after hemodynamic stability is achieved  
– Bicarbonate infusion is not indicated for a mild metabolic acidosis (HCO₃⁻ 22)  
– Calcium gluconate is not indicated here  

Final Answer:  
The most appropriate next step is infusion of isotonic saline.  

\boxed{0.9\% \text{ Saline}}

Step 1: Identify the clinical scenario  
• 23-year-old woman collapses at the end of a marathon, feels dizzy/light-headed but remains alert.  
• No headache or nausea, no prior medical problems.  

Step 2: Review vital signs and exam  
• Pulse 120/min, BP 85/50 mm Hg → tachycardia + hypotension  
• No other abnormal findings  

Step 3: Initial management and response  
• Feet elevated, IV normal saline begun  
• 30 minutes later: pulse 70/min, BP 110/70 mm Hg; patient feels well  

Step 4: Pathophysiology  
• During exercise: ↑cutaneous and splanchnic blood flow + muscle pump activity maintains venous return.  
• At exercise cessation: muscle pump stops, peripheral vessels still dilated → blood pools in legs → ↓venous return → ↓cardiac output → transient hypotension and syncope.  
• Elevating legs and giving saline restores preload and corrects hypotension.  

Step 5: Match to answer choices  
(A) Autonomic dysfunction – unlikely; autonomic reflexes intact (tachycardia responded).  
(B) Hypokalemia – would cause muscle weakness/cramps, not acute hypotension.  
(C) Hyponatremia – would cause neurologic symptoms (headache, nausea, seizures), not isolated hypotension.  
(D) Myocardial inflammation – would present with chest pain, heart failure signs, not rapid improvement with fluids.  
(E) Orthostatic hypotension – fits sudden drop in blood pressure on standing/exercise cessation that corrects with leg elevation and volume repletion.

Final answer: \boxed{Orthostatic\ hypotension} (E)

Step 1: Identify the clinical setting  
– A term (41 wks) parturient in the second stage of labor.  

Step 2: Note the acute presentation  
– After 10 min of pushing, there is a sudden prolonged fetal deceleration, then the mother develops acute shortness of breath, cyanosis, and becomes unresponsive with no detectable pulse or blood pressure.  

Step 3: Recognize ensuing coagulopathy  
– Five minutes into resuscitation, bleeding appears from mucosal sites and IV lines, indicating disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC).  

Step 4: Match the triad to the most likely cause  
– The combination of sudden cardiovascular collapse, respiratory distress, and DIC during labor is characteristic of an amniotic fluid embolism.  

Other choices are less consistent:  
– Intracerebral hemorrhage: would present with neurologic signs but not acute DIC.  
– Myocardial infarction: rare in a young healthy woman and doesn’t cause DIC.  
– Status asthmaticus: would present with bronchospasm without DIC or collapse.  
– Toxic shock syndrome: requires staphylococcal toxin and presents with fever, rash, and shock over days, not sudden collapse in labor.  

Final Answer  
\boxed{A}

Step 1: Define potency and efficacy  
- Potency refers to the concentration (or dose) of drug needed to produce 50% of the maximal effect (EC₅₀). A more potent drug has a lower EC₅₀ and its curve is shifted to the left.  
- Efficacy refers to the maximal effect (E_max) a drug can produce, regardless of dose. A more efficacious drug has a higher plateau on the concentration-response curve.

Step 2: Compare the three curves  
- Identify which curve is leftmost (lowest EC₅₀) → that drug is most potent.  
- Identify which curve reaches highest maximum response → that drug has the greatest efficacy.

Step 3: Apply to Drugs X, Y, and Z  
- Drug X’s curve is furthest to the left, indicating it has the lowest EC₅₀ → highest potency.  
- All three curves plateau at the same maximal level (no drug exceeds that plateau) → they have equal efficacy.

Step 4: Choose the answer  
Since Drug X requires the lowest concentration to achieve its effect (most potent) and none of the drugs has a higher maximal effect than the others (efficacies are equal), the best description is:

\boxed{A}  Drug X is more potent than Drugs Y or Z.

Step 1: The key findings are cyanosis, low pulse‐oximetry (82%) despite normal lung fields and no murmur, and a chocolate‐brown blood sample.  
Step 2: Chocolate‐brown blood that does not correct with oxygen suggests methemoglobinemia (heme iron in Fe3+ state can’t bind O2).  
Step 3: The most common cause of congenital methemoglobinemia is decreased activity of NADH‐dependent methemoglobin reductase (cytochrome b5 reductase), which normally reduces Fe3+ back to Fe2+.  

Therefore, the answer is  
\boxed{A} Decreased activity in the enzyme that reduces Fe^{3+} to Fe^{2+}.

Step 1: Recognize the initial presentation as diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)  
‐ 12-year-old with type I DM, dehydration (dry mucosa, poor turgor), hypotension, tachycardia  
‐ Labs: glucose ≈ 560 mg/dL, metabolic acidosis (pH 7.20, HCO₃⁻ ≈ 10), anion gap ↑

Step 2: Standard therapy for DKA  
‐ Isotonic (0.9%) saline boluses to restore intravascular volume and perfusion  
‐ Continuous IV insulin infusion to correct hyperglycemia and stop ketogenesis  
‐ Monitor and replace electrolytes (especially potassium)

Step 3: New neurologic symptoms during treatment  
‐ After 3 h of fluids and insulin she becomes somnolent, develops headache, has emesis and incontinence  
‐ Urine output is low (30 mL/h)  
‐ These findings in a child receiving DKA therapy are most consistent with cerebral edema

Step 4: Treatment of DKA-associated cerebral edema  
‐ Immediate measures: raise the head of the bed, reduce IV fluids rate, intubate if necessary  
‐ Pharmacologic therapy: IV mannitol (0.5–1 g/kg over 20 min) or hypertonic saline  
‐ Mannitol creates an osmotic gradient to draw fluid out of the brain and reduce intracranial pressure

Step 5: Rule out the other options  
‐ (A) Bicarbonate: generally avoided in pediatric DKA (can worsen cerebral edema)  
‐ (B) Dexamethasone: used for vasogenic edema (tumor, abscess), not DKA cerebral edema  
‐ (C) 50% dextrose: used to treat hypoglycemia, not indicated here  
‐ (D) Furosemide: not first‐line for intracranial hypertension in this setting

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Mannitol}}

Step 1: Identify key features  
– Nonresolving pneumonia despite appropriate antibiotics (ceftriaxone + azithromycin)  
– Hilar adenopathy on chest x‐ray  
– Marked eosinophilia (15% of normal WBC count)  
– Recent work in the southwestern United States (endemic area)  
– Negative PPD and AFB smears (rules out active TB)

Step 2: Narrow the differential  
– Eosinophilia + pneumonia in Southwest U.S. → Think coccidioidomycosis (“Valley fever”)  
– Other choices (Legionella antigen, bacterial culture, CT scan, fungal blood culture, sputum cytology) are either non‐specific or less sensitive for this diagnosis  

Step 3: Choose the most appropriate diagnostic test  
– The best initial and most sensitive test for coccidioidomycosis is serologic testing for antibodies to Coccidioides immitis.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Serologic study for Coccidioides immitis (D)}}

Step 1: Identify the clinical picture  
– A 62-year-old man with urinary hesitancy, dribbling, and nocturia  
– Physical exam: diffusely enlarged, firm, nontender prostate  
→ Classic presentation of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)

Step 2: Recall pathogenesis of BPH  
– Stromal and epithelial cell hyperplasia in the prostate periurethral zone  
– Driven by dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which binds androgen receptors in prostatic cells and stimulates growth  

Step 3: Recall how DHT is produced  
– Testosterone → (5α-reductase) → DHT  
– DHT is more potent than testosterone at the androgen receptor in prostate tissue  

Step 4: Evaluate answer choices  
(A) α1-adrenergic receptor activation → increases smooth muscle tone in prostate/bladder neck, worsens urinary symptoms but does not cause hyperplasia  
(B) Conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone → key driver of prostatic cell proliferation in BPH  
(C) Conversion of testosterone to estradiol → estradiol may have minor roles but is not the principal cause of BPH  
(D) Inhibition of α1-adrenergic receptor → used to treat symptoms, does not cause BPH  
(E) Production of prostate-specific antigen → marker of prostate activity but not a causative factor  

Step 5: Choose the best answer  
The major factor contributing to the development of BPH is the intraprostatic conversion of testosterone to the more potent androgen DHT.

Final answer: \boxed{Conversion\ of\ testosterone\ to\ dihydrotestosterone} (Choice B)

Step 1: Identify the key finding  
– The patient’s serum glucose is elevated at 220 mg/dL on day 1 after major trauma.

Step 2: Consider potential causes of acute hyperglycemia in an ICU trauma patient  
– Exogenous glucose load (the patient is receiving 5% dextrose in IV fluids)  
– Stress‐induced hyperglycemia (catecholamines, cortisol, glucagon ↑ → insulin resistance & ↑ gluconeogenesis)  
– Renal impairment (↓ glucose excretion)  
– True insulin deficiency (e.g. new‐onset type 1 diabetes)  
– Bacterial contamination producing glucose (not a recognized mechanism)

Step 3: Evaluate each choice  
(A) Decreased uptake of glucose by insulin‐sensitive tissues  
 – In acute trauma/stress, catecholamines and cortisol rise ⇒ peripheral insulin resistance ⇒ ↓ glucose uptake in muscle and fat  
(B) Exogenous production of glucose by bacteria  
 – Not clinically relevant  
(C) Impaired excretion of glucose by the kidneys  
 – Kidneys are normal (creatinine 1 mg/dL, no glycosuria mechanism here)  
(D) Increased infusion of intravenous glucose  
 – A 5% dextrose infusion provides only 5 g glucose per 100 mL; routine maintenance rates rarely raise glucose this high by themselves  
(E) Insulin deficiency  
 – No history of diabetes; hyperglycemia is stress‐induced rather than true insulin deficiency  

Step 4: Choose the most likely mechanism  
– The acute rise in glucose in a trauma patient on day 1 is best explained by stress‐mediated insulin resistance (↓ peripheral glucose uptake).

Final Answer:  
\boxed{A} Decreased uptake of glucose by insulin‐sensitive tissues

Step 1: Recognize the murmur and clinical presentation  
– The patient has an irregularly irregular pulse at 130/min (atrial fibrillation) plus a diastolic “rumble” best heard at the apex.  
– An apical diastolic rumble in a middle‐aged woman with a history of childhood infections strongly suggests rheumatic mitral stenosis leading to left atrial enlargement and secondary atrial fibrillation.  

Step 2: Identify the next most useful test among the choices  
– Echocardiography would be the gold standard to confirm mitral stenosis and assess severity, but it is not offered.  
– Chest X-ray can show left atrial enlargement and pulmonary venous hypertension but is less directly diagnostic.  
– ECG will (a) document and characterize her atrial fibrillation, (b) demonstrate evidence of left atrial enlargement (e.g. P-mitrale), and (c) guide rate‐control therapy.  
– ASO titer and CRP are not useful in a chronic rheumatic process.  
– TSH is only indicated if hyperthyroidism is suspected, but the loud diastolic rumble clearly points to structural mitral disease, not a primary thyroid disorder.  

Therefore, the single most helpful diagnostic study at this point is an ECG.  

Final answer: \boxed{\text{ECG (choice B)}}

Step 1: Recognize the presentation  
– Premature (30 wk) neonate, now 2 wk old, with temperature instability, feeding intolerance (gastric residuals), abdominal distension, blood-streaked stools, leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, hypotension.  
– These findings are classic for necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC).

Step 2: Recall the diagnostic study for NEC  
– The hallmark radiographic finding in NEC is pneumatosis intestinalis (air in the bowel wall) seen on plain abdominal X-ray.  
– Other signs may include portal venous gas and free air if perforation has occurred.

Step 3: Eliminate the incorrect choices  
(A) PT/PTT – assesses coagulation (eg, DIC) but does not confirm NEC  
(B) Stool reducing substances – tests for carbohydrate malabsorption, not NEC  
(C) Stool culture – checks for infection, not diagnostic for NEC  
(D) Upper GI series – used for malrotation/volvulus, not NEC  

Step 4: Select the best test  
– Plain abdominal radiograph is the study of choice to confirm NEC.

Final Answer: \(\boxed{\text{X-ray of the abdomen}}\)

Step 1: Identify the MRI findings.  
The MRI (sagittal T2‐weighted) shows decreased disc height and signal intensity at multiple lumbar levels, consistent with degenerative disc disease.

Step 2: List risk factors for degenerative disc disease.  
Major contributors include:  
– Age (disc degeneration increases with age)  
– Obesity (increased mechanical load on the spine)  
– Smoking (impaired microvascular perfusion of the disc, accelerates degeneration)  
– Heavy alcohol use, certain medications (not typically implicated in disc degeneration)

Step 3: Review the patient’s history for these factors.  
– Age: 57 years  
– Obesity: BMI 33 kg/m2  
– Smoking: 2 packs/day for 25 years  
– Alcohol: 2 beers daily, but none for past 5 years  
– Medications: diclofenac (NSAID), loratadine (antihistamine)  
– Past opioid use: stopped 5 years ago

Step 4: Determine which factor most strongly contributes to accelerated disc degeneration.  
Cigarette smoking is well‐established to impair nutrient diffusion to intervertebral discs, leading to earlier and more severe degenerative changes on MRI.

Final Answer: \boxed{B}

Step 1: Localize the fracture on the X-ray

Her recurrent, hours-long spells of vertigo accompanied by tinnitus, ear fullness, and hearing loss are classic for Meniere’s disease.

Step 1: Identify the vessels supplying the liver.

  – Falciform ligament attaches the liver to the anterior abdominal wall.

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– A pruritic, erythematous, vesicular rash developing a few days after exposure to a new hair dye is classic for allergic (type IV) contact dermatitis.

Step 2: Recall the immunologic mechanism  
– Hapten (hair‐dye chemical) binds skin proteins → processed by epidermal Langerhans cells → they migrate to regional lymph nodes → present antigen via MHC II to naïve CD4⁺ T cells.  
– Two signals are needed for full T‐cell activation and proliferation:  
  • Signal 1: T-cell receptor (on CD4⁺ cell) binds antigen-MHC II complex.  
  • Signal 2 (costimulation): CD28 on the T cell binds B7 molecules (CD80/CD86) on the antigen-presenting cell (Langerhans cell).

Step 3: Match ligand–receptor pairs in the answer choices  
(A) CD2 → ICAM-3: adhesion, not primary costimulation  
(B) CD28 → CD80: classic costimulatory signal (Signal 2) for T-cell proliferation  
(C) CD40L → CD40: B-cell activation and macrophage activation, not primary for naïve T-cell proliferation  
(D) CD45 → CD28: incorrect pairing (CD45 is a tyrosine phosphatase on leukocytes)  
(E) LFA-1 → ICAM1: adhesion/interleukin-2 facilitation, but not the major costimulatory signal

Step 4: Choose the best answer  
– The critical costimulatory interaction driving T‐cell proliferation in delayed hypersensitivity is CD28 on T cells binding B7 (CD80/CD86) on APCs (here, Langerhans cells).

Final Answer: \boxed{(B)\;CD28\text{ on T lymphocytes} \rightarrow CD80\text{ on epidermal Langerhans cells}}

Step 1: Define α and β  
- α (type I error rate) is set by the investigator (here 0.05). It does **not** change with sample size.  
- β (type II error rate) is the probability of missing a true difference; power = 1 – β.

Step 2: Understand effect of sample size  
- Larger n → greater precision → higher power (lower β)  
- Smaller n → less precision → lower power (higher β)

Step 3: Apply to the question  
- Reducing the sample size from 1000 to 500 will **decrease** power and thus **increase** β (type II error rate).

Answer:  
\boxed{B}

Step 1: Identify the clinical problem  
A 25-year-old woman has severe crampy lower abdominal pain that begins with the onset of menstruation and lasts for the duration of her period. There is no mention of pelvic pathology (e.g., endometriosis, fibroids), suggesting primary dysmenorrhea.

Step 2: Pathogenesis of primary dysmenorrhea  
Primary dysmenorrhea is caused by excessive production of uterine prostaglandins (especially PGF2α) during menstruation. These prostaglandins increase uterine tone and contractions, leading to ischemia and pain.

Step 3: First‐line pharmacotherapy  
Nonsteroidal anti‐inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are the initial treatment of choice for primary dysmenorrhea. They relieve pain by blocking cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2), which are necessary for prostaglandin synthesis.

Step 4: Match mechanism to answer choices  
(A) Inhibition of estrogen synthesis – used for estrogen‐dependent conditions, not first‐line for dysmenorrhea.  
(B) Inhibition of 11β‐hydroxylase activity – this affects adrenal steroid synthesis, irrelevant here.  
(C) Inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis – exactly how NSAIDs work to relieve dysmenorrhea.  
(D) Stimulation of follicle‐stimulating hormone synthesis – would promote ovarian follicle growth, not analgesic.  
(E) Stimulation of luteinizing hormone synthesis – would trigger ovulation, not relevant to pain relief.

Final Answer:  
The initial pharmacotherapy (NSAIDs) works by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis.  

\boxed{C}

Step 2: Determine first-line therapy

\boxed{Ampicillin\text{-}sulbactam}

Step 1: Characterize the murmur  
– It is a systolic murmur with a palpable thrill at the base of the heart → suggests a high‐grade, high‐flow obstruction across the aortic valve (i.e., aortic stenosis, AS).

Step 2: Apply maneuver effects  
– Valsalva (strain phase) ↓ venous return → ↓ intensity of AS murmur  
– Handgrip ↑ afterload → ↓ pressure gradient across a stenotic aortic valve → ↓ AS murmur  
– Squatting ↑ venous return and ↑ afterload → ↑ flow and ↑ gradient across a stenotic valve → ↑ AS murmur  

These findings exactly match aortic stenosis.

Step 3: Exclude other lesions  
– Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) increases with Valsalva and decreases with squatting (opposite of what is seen here).  
– Mitral regurgitation (MR) increases with handgrip.  
– Aortic insufficiency, mitral stenosis are diastolic murmurs and do not fit a systolic thrill at the base.

Final answer: \boxed{Aortic\ stenosis} (choice B)

Step 1. Identify the defect  
– The patient has pernicious anemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → B₁₂ deficiency).  
– Laboratory findings: ↑ methylmalonic acid, ↑ homocysteine  

Step 2. Recall B₁₂‐dependent reactions  
1) Methylmalonyl‐CoA mutase: methylmalonyl‐CoA → succinyl‐CoA  
2) Methionine synthase: homocysteine → methionine  

Step 3. Link to amino acid synthesis  
– In B₁₂ deficiency, methionine synthase activity falls → homocysteine accumulates and methionine cannot be regenerated.  

Step 4. Choose the amino acid whose synthesis is most impaired  
(A) Cysteine – derived from homocysteine via cystathionine β‐synthase (PLP dependent), not directly B₁₂ dependent  
(B) Glutamine – metabolically unrelated to B₁₂  
(C) Methionine – requires B₁₂ for its synthesis from homocysteine  
(D) Phenylalanine – hydroxylated to tyrosine (BH₄ dependent), not B₁₂ dependent  
(E) Tyrosine – derived from phenylalanine, not B₁₂ dependent  

Therefore, the synthesis most impaired is methionine.  

Final Answer: \boxed{C}

Step 1: Define the defense mechanisms  
- Countertransference: the therapist’s projected feelings onto the patient (not relevant to the patient’s behavior).  
- Projection: attributing one’s own unacceptable thoughts or feelings to someone else.  
- Projective identification: pushing unwanted parts of the self into another person, who then starts to behave in accordance with those projected feelings.  
- Reaction formation: converting an unacceptable impulse into its opposite.  
- Splitting: viewing people or situations in “all-good” or “all-bad” terms, with no middle ground.

Step 2: Analyze the patient’s behavior  
- He is polite and respectful to the physician (“all good”) but berates nurses and staff (“all bad”).  
- He praises two children (“good”) and denigrates the third child as “worthless” (“bad”).  

This black-and-white thinking—alternating between idealizing and devaluing others—is the hallmark of splitting.

Final Answer: \boxed{E} Splitting.

Step 1: Clinical presentation  
– Previously healthy young adult with acute fever, headache, cough → now has seizure, postictal confusion, and focal cognitive deficits (naming difficulty, paraphasic errors).  

Step 2: Neuroimaging  
– MRI shows bitemporal hyperintensities, a pattern classically seen with involvement of the temporal lobes.  

Step 3: CSF findings  
– Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (121 WBC/mm³, 88% monocytes), elevated protein (78 mg/dL), and notable erythrocytes (340 RBC/mm³) suggesting a hemorrhagic component.  

Step 4: Differential diagnosis  
– Bacterial meningitis: typically neutrophilic pleocytosis, very high protein, low glucose, no isolated temporal lobe lesions or RBCs.  
– Dextromethorphan intoxication: may cause confusion but not focal seizures or temporal lobe MRI changes.  
– HIV encephalopathy: subacute, diffuse cognitive decline without marked fever, seizures, or focal temporal lesions.  
– Reye syndrome: occurs in children after aspirin use, presents with acute encephalopathy and liver dysfunction.  
– Syphilis: neurosyphilis is more chronic, with variable CSF VDRL, not acute hemorrhagic temporal lobe lesions.  

Step 5: Most likely diagnosis  
Herpes simplex virus type 1 encephalitis targets the temporal lobes, causes hemorrhagic necrosis (RBCs in CSF), lymphocytic pleocytosis, fever, seizures, and focal deficits.  

Final answer: \boxed{Herpes\ simplex\ encephalitis\ (C)}

– Undescended testes have a risk of gonadal malignancy (seminoma)

– The next appropriate step is to remove the gonads to prevent malignancy.

Step 1: Identify the fracture from the history and CT scan  
– The patient fell onto the left zygomatic arch and now has periorbital ecchymoses and diplopia.  
– The CT (axial) shows a fracture of the orbital floor/infraorbital rim (a “blow-out” component of a zygomaticomaxillary complex fracture).

Step 2: Recall the anatomy of the orbital floor and rim  
– The infraorbital groove on the orbital floor leads into the infraorbital canal in the maxilla.  
– The infraorbital nerve (branch of V₂) and infraorbital artery (branch of the maxillary artery) run together in this groove/canal.  
– A fracture here commonly injures both the nerve (causing numbness of the cheek, upper lip) and the artery.

Step 3: Eliminate other choices  
(A) Facial artery – runs more superficially along the mandible and cheek; not in the orbital floor.  
(B) Frontal artery – a branch of the ophthalmic artery (supraorbital or supratrochlear) runs on the forehead; not in the infraorbital canal.  
(D) Lacrimal artery – branch of the ophthalmic artery supplying the lacrimal gland in the superolateral orbit.  
(E) Ophthalmic artery – enters the orbit via the optic canal; gives off supraorbital and lacrimal branches but does not run in the infraorbital groove.

Step 4: Choose the artery that courses through the fractured area  
– Infraorbital artery travels in the infraorbital groove/canal in the orbital floor and is at greatest risk.

Final Answer: \boxed{Infraorbital\ artery\ (C)}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical picture  
The patient has:  
  • 10-month history of wide red (purple) striae on the trunk  
  • Central weight gain (face “moon,” abdomen) with thin extremities  
  • Increased appetite  
These findings are classic for Cushing syndrome (chronic glucocorticoid excess).

Step 2: Recall effects of excess cortisol  
  • Increases vascular sensitivity to catecholamines → hypertension  
  • Promotes protein catabolism in muscle → proximal muscle weakness  
  • Raises blood glucose (gluconeogenesis) → hyperglycemia  
  • Causes thinning of skin, easy bruising, striae  
  • Causes hypokalemia (mineralocorticoid activity)

Step 3: Match with answer choices  
(A) Hyperkalemia – wrong (cortisol→hypokalemia), though bruising is seen  
(B) Hypertension and muscle weakness – correct  
(C) Hypocalcemia and hyperglycemia – cortisol causes hyperglycemia but not classically hypocalcemia  
(D) Hypoglycemia – wrong (cortisol→hyperglycemia)  
(E) Jaundice – not a feature of Cushing syndrome  

Final Answer: \boxed{Hypertension\ and\ muscle\ weakness\ (B)}

Step 1: Identify key features  
– Angioedema of face, lips, tongue without urticaria or pruritus  
– Onset 6 weeks after starting an ACE inhibitor  
– Recurrent episodes resolving over 1–3 days  

Step 2: Recall mechanisms of angioedema  
– Histamine-mediated (allergic) angioedema → usually with urticaria, pruritus, rapid onset/resolution  
– Bradykinin-mediated angioedema → often ACE inhibitors (↓ breakdown of bradykinin), no urticaria, slower resolution  

Step 3: Link ACE inhibitors to bradykinin  
– ACE (kininase II) normally degrades bradykinin  
– ACE inhibitors block this degradation → accumulation of bradykinin → increased vascular permeability → angioedema  

Step 4: Choose the mediator  
Among the options, bradykinin best explains ACE-inhibitor–induced angioedema without itching or hives.  

Final Answer:  \boxed{Bradykinin}

Step 1: Lisinopril is an ACE inhibitor, so it ↓ angiotensin II levels.  
Step 2: Angiotensin II preferentially constricts the efferent arteriole. With less Ang II, the efferent arteriole dilates.  
Step 3: Efferent dilation → ↓ glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure → ↓ GFR.  
Step 4: Efferent dilation also ↓ overall resistance to outflow, so RPF ↑.  
Step 5: Filtration fraction (FF = GFR/RPF) therefore ↓.  

So the pattern is  
GFR ↓, RPF ↑, FF ↓  
which corresponds to choice (D).  

Final answer: \boxed{D}

– An acute lunate dislocation is an orthopedic emergency. It requires prompt reduction (closed if possible but often open) under appropriate sedation/anesthesia. Definitive management is by an orthopedic or hand surgeon.

Step 1: Recall the role of cardiac neural crest cells  
- These cells migrate into the outflow tract (truncus arteriosus) and bulbus cordis.  
- They contribute to formation of the aorticopulmonary (AP) septum, which divides the single outflow tract into the ascending aorta and pulmonary trunk.

Step 2: Predict the consequence of absent cardiac neural crest cells  
- Without these cells, the AP septum cannot form.  
- The truncus arteriosus remains undivided, so a single great vessel leaves the heart.

Step 3: Identify the corresponding congenital defect  
- Persistent truncus arteriosus is defined by failure of the truncus arteriosus to divide into aorta and pulmonary trunk.  
- This defect directly results from lack of neural crest–derived septation of the outflow tract.

Step 4: Eliminate the other choices  
(A) Atrial septal defect – involves endocardial cushion or septum primum/secondum, not neural crest  
(B) Coarctation of the aorta – often due to abnormal development of the aortic arch, but not directly from absent cardiac neural crest  
(C) Mitral stenosis – usually rheumatic or leaflet abnormality, unrelated to neural crest  
(E) Transposition of the great arteries – results from abnormal spiraling of the AP septum but not complete failure to form it

Final Answer: \boxed{Persistent\ truncus\ arteriosus\ (D)}

Step 1: Identify the event rates  
• Risk with Procedure A = 12 cases per 1000 = 12/1000 = 0.012  
• Risk with Procedure B = 18 cases per 1000 = 18/1000 = 0.018  

Step 2: Recall definition of absolute risk reduction (ARR)  
ARR = Risk​(control) – Risk​(treatment)  

Here, “control” is Procedure B and “treatment” is Procedure A.  

Step 3: Calculate ARR  
ARR = (18/1000) – (12/1000)  
     = 6/1000  
     = 0.006 (or 0.6%)  

Thus the best estimate of the absolute risk reduction is (18/1000) – (12/1000).  

Final Answer: \boxed{(18/1000)\;-\;(12/1000)}

Step 1: Assess the contraction pattern  
– Contractions every 2 minutes lasting 60 seconds = 30 contractions in 60 minutes = 5 in 10 minutes.  
– This exceeds the normal upper limit (≤5 contractions per 10 minutes), so this is uterine tachysystole (hyperstimulation).  

Step 2: Identify the deceleration type on the tracing  
– The decelerations begin after the peak of each contraction and recover after the contraction ends → these are “late” decelerations.  
– Late decelerations reflect uteroplacental insufficiency: during the contraction the uterus compresses the spiral arteries, reducing fetal oxygenation; if contractions are too frequent (hyperstimulation), oxygen delivery falls further, producing late decelerations.

Step 3: Exclude other choices  
– Occult shoulder dystocia → would present with variable or early decels and difficulty delivering the shoulders, not a pattern of consistent late decels.  
– Umbilical cord prolapse → produces sudden, deep variable decelerations, often with an obvious drop in baseline rate, not uniform late decels.  
– Uterine rupture → would present with loss of contractions, sudden fetal distress/bradycardia, maternal pain/bleeding, and loss of station.  
– Chronic uteroplacental insufficiency (e.g. pre‐eclampsia) causes late decelerations but is gradual and present before induction; here the late decels develop acutely with oxytocin hyperstimulation.

Conclusion: Excess oxytocin has produced uterine hyperstimulation, causing uteroplacental insufficiency and late decelerations.  

Final answer: \boxed{C\;Uterine\;hyperstimulation}

– Historically, concern existed that opioids might “mask” physical findings and delay diagnosis.

(B) Gabapentin – not indicated for acute nociceptive abdominal pain.  
(C) Lorazepam – an anxiolytic/sedative; does not treat nociceptive pain.  
(D) Morphine – an opioid analgesic appropriate for moderate–severe pain; does not obscure exam findings to a clinically significant degree.  
(E) Explaining that analgesics would interfere with the exam – outdated and unethical; pain relief should not be withheld.

Step 5: Choose the most appropriate next step.  
– Administering morphine is both safe (does not meaningfully obscure the exam) and humane (treats his significant pain).

Step 1: Identify the key facts  
– Patient is an 8-year-old minor with massive intra-abdominal hemorrhage, hemodynamically unstable (BP 50/20, HR 210), hemoglobin 3.8 g/dL, and requires immediate blood transfusion to survive.  
– Parents have given consent for surgery but refuse blood products on religious grounds.  

Step 2: Recall the relevant legal/ethical principles  
– Parents may generally consent for or refuse treatment for their minor children, but that authority is not absolute.  
– When a child’s life is threatened and parents refuse a life-saving intervention, physicians may override that refusal under the doctrine of parens patriae.  
– In a true emergency, physicians may proceed with necessary treatment without a formal court order; however, they should obtain an emergency (telephone) court order as soon as possible to document the decision.

Step 3: Evaluate the answer choices  
A. Contact the national organization of the parents’ religion – delays life-saving care and is not required.  
B. Contact the parents’ religious advisor – also delays care and is not binding.  
C. Proceed with the blood transfusion while obtaining an emergency telephone court order – overrides the parents’ refusal in a life-threatening emergency, secures legal authorization, and does not delay care.  
D. Abide by the parents’ refusal – would likely result in the child’s death and is not legally or ethically permissible in this emergency.  
E. Transfer to a bloodless surgical center – impractical and would cause lethal delay.

Step 4: Make the best choice  
The most appropriate action is to override the parental refusal in order to save the child’s life and to obtain an emergency telephone court order immediately afterward to document and authorize the transfusion.

Final Answer  
\boxed{C} Proceed with the blood transfusion while obtaining an emergency telephone court order.

Step 1: Summarize the key features

– Goodpasture’s (anti–GBM): pulmonary hemorrhage + renal involvement, but no neuropathy  
– Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener’s): pulmonary lesions + renal disease, but neuropathy is less prominent  
– Microscopic polyangiitis: similar to Wegener’s, again renal > neurologic  
– Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN): mononeuritis multiplex + GI, renal ischemia, but typically no pulmonary hemorrhage  
– Sarcoidosis: can have pulmonary infiltrates + neuropathy (e.g. facial nerve), but hemoptysis is unusual  
– Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss): asthma history, pulmonary infiltrates/hemorrhage, eosinophilia (often high IgE), and mononeuritis multiplex  

Step 3: Most likely diagnosis  
– Churg-Strauss (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) fits asthma + pulmonary hemorrhage + mononeuritis multiplex  

Step 4: Appropriate diagnostic test  
– Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis is strongly associated with p-ANCA (anti-myeloperoxidase ANCA)  
– Serum IgE is often elevated but is nonspecific—ANCA testing is preferred to confirm a small-vessel vasculitis  

Final answer  
The next best diagnostic step is serum antineutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibody assay.

Step-by-step reasoning:

1. Time course of post-absorptive glucose maintenance  
   - In the first few hours after a meal (postprandial), glucose is maintained by continued absorption and insulin‐mediated uptake.  
   - After ~4–6 hours, absorption is complete, and the body switches to the post-absorptive (fasting) state.  
   - In the early fasting state (up to ~12–18 hours), the liver maintains blood glucose primarily by breaking down its glycogen stores (glycogenolysis) and by some gluconeogenesis.

2. Evaluate each choice  
 (A) Continued gut absorption of calories from the ingested meal  
   – After 12 hours without eating, virtually all ingested nutrients have been absorbed. Not a major contributor.  

 (B) Glucose release from skeletal muscle  
   – Skeletal muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase, so it cannot export free glucose; it makes lactate/alanine for hepatic gluconeogenesis but does not directly release glucose.  

 (C) Glycogenolysis in the liver  
   – The liver’s glycogen stores are the primary source of blood glucose in the early fasting period (up to ~12–18 h). This is the main mechanism 12 hours after a meal.  

 (D) Increased leptin release from adipose tissues  
   – Leptin regulates appetite and long-term energy balance, not acute maintenance of blood glucose.  

 (E) Inhibition of glucagon release by the pancreas  
   – In fasting, glucagon is actually increased (not inhibited) to stimulate hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis.

3. Conclusion  
   After 12 hours of fasting in a healthy individual, hepatic glycogenolysis is the predominant mechanism maintaining serum glucose.

Final answer: \boxed{C}

– The initial imaging study for suspected acalculous cholecystitis is right upper‐quadrant ultrasonography.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Ultrasonography of the abdomen (D)}}

– A patient with a history of heavy, long‐term alcohol use who is now requesting treatment for alcohol withdrawal should be treated with a benzodiazepine (eg, diazepam, lorazepam) to prevent seizures and delirium tremens.

Step 2: Recall the mechanism of benzodiazepines  
– Benzodiazepines are positive allosteric modulators of the GABAA receptor.  
– They bind to a distinct site on the GABAA receptor complex and increase the frequency of chloride channel opening in response to GABA, enhancing GABA’s inhibitory effect.

(F) Inhibition of serotonin reuptake → SSRIs  
(G) Opening of glutamate channels → excitatory, opposite of benzo action  
(H) Stimulation of 5-hydroxytryptophan receptors → not applicable

The correct choice is (C).

Step 2: Understand the coagulopathy of cirrhosis  
– The liver synthesizes most clotting factors (I, II, V, VII, IX, X) and regulatory proteins (protein C, protein S)  
– In cirrhosis, hepatic synthetic function is impaired, leading to decreased levels of these factors and prolongation of PT/INR

– Among the vitamin K–dependent factors, factor VII has the shortest half-life (~4–6 hours)  
– A decrease in factor VII causes an early and marked prolongation of the PT

(D) Inhibition of vitamin K epoxide reductase  ← mechanism of warfarin, not cirrhosis  
(E) Splenic sequestration  ← lowers platelet count but does not explain the marked PT prolongation  

Final answer: the predominant cause of his coagulopathy and bleeding risk is decreased hepatic production of factor VII.

(D) IV furosemide – may relieve volume overload but does not treat the underlying crescentic GN  
(E) IV methylprednisolone – correct, high-dose steroids are required to arrest crescent formation and preserve renal function

Step 1: Recognize respiratory distress in a preterm newborn

Step 3: Recall embryology of the diaphragm  
– The diaphragm is formed by:  
  • Septum transversum (central tendon)  
  • Pleuroperitoneal membranes (folds)  
  • Dorsal mesentery of the esophagus  
  • Body wall musculature  
– A posterolateral (Bochdalek) hernia results from failure of the pleuroperitoneal membranes (folds) to fuse with the other components, allowing abdominal contents to herniate into the thorax.

(C) Fibrous pericardium – derived from pleuropericardial membranes; not related to posterolateral hernia  
(D) Pleuroperitoneal fold – failure of this to develop/fuse causes a Bochdalek hernia  

Final answer: \boxed{Pleuroperitoneal\ fold\ (D)}

Step 1:  Identify the phases on the graph  
- After the first (primary) immunization there is a “lag phase” before any detectable antibody appears; then antibody titers rise to Peak A and fall off.  
- After the second (secondary) immunization there is a much shorter lag and a higher Peak B.  

Step 2:  Understand what causes the primary lag phase (marked with the asterisk)  
- Immediately after antigen injection, the immune system must:  
  1. Take up and process antigen  
  2. Present antigen to naïve B cells (with T-cell help for a T-dependent antigen)  
  3. Drive clonal selection and proliferation of those antigen-specific B cells  
  4. Differentiate them into antibody-secreting plasma cells  

Step 3:  Match the steps to the answer choices  
(A) Antigen recognition – necessary, but not sufficient to explain the several‐day delay  
(B) Apoptosis of activated lymphocytes – occurs after the peak (contraction phase), not in the lag  
(C) B-lymphocyte expansion – the proliferation/differentiation of antigen-specific B cells into plasma cells is the rate-limiting step in the lag period  
(D) Macrophage phagocytosis – part of innate defense, does not directly set the length of the lag in antibody production  
(E) T-lymphocyte activation – important for a T-dependent response, but the major time sink is the clonal expansion of B cells  

Therefore, the lag (asterisk) before antibody appears is directly due to the clonal expansion of B cells.  

Final Answer: \boxed{C}

– No growth in the presence of bacitracin (bacitracin sensitivity) → the organism is inhibited by bacitracin.

– S. agalactiae (Group B) = bacitracin resistant (not inhibited)

Step 4: Correlate all data  
– β‐hemolysis + bacitracin sensitivity → S. pyogenes (Group A)

Final Answer  
\boxed{S.\ pyogenes\ (group\ A)}

(A) Publication bias – applies to the literature as a whole, not a single cohort study’s internal validity.  
(B) Selection bias – in a nonrandomized cohort, patients may differ systematically beyond matched variables (e.g., physicians may preferentially prescribe the new drug to patients perceived at higher risk, or patients more likely to report fatigue). This can invalidate the comparison.  
(C) Type I error – false positive; with p = 0.01 the chance of a Type I error is low (1%).  
(D) Type II error – false negative; not relevant because the study found a statistically significant difference.

Step 3: Choose the most likely flaw  
Because treatment assignment was not randomized and was based on clinical decision‐making, there is a high risk that the two groups differ in ways other than blood pressure, age, and gender. This is selection bias.

– She binge‐eats and then forcefully vomits (“purges”) nearly every day → classic bulimia nervosa.  
– Vomiting causes loss of gastric HCl → metabolic alkalosis (“contraction alkalosis” from volume depletion).

– Serum K⁺: decreased  
– Serum Cl⁻: decreased  
– Serum HCO₃⁻: increased  

Answer choice matching “↓ K⁺, ↓ Cl⁻, ↑ HCO₃⁻” is choice B.  

Final answer: \boxed{B} (decreased, decreased, increased)

Step 1: Identify the key clinical features

- Full-thickness necrosis of keratinocytes throughout the epidermis

Step 2: Recall management principles for femoral-neck fractures  
– Femoral-neck fractures disrupt the blood supply to the femoral head (risk of avascular necrosis).  
– In elderly patients with displaced femoral-neck fractures, arthroplasty (hemi- or total hip replacement) is often performed.  
– In young patients, the goal is to preserve the native femoral head, so one performs prompt anatomic reduction and stable internal fixation to promote healing and minimize AVN risk.

(B) Closed reduction followed by traction – outdated; traction alone won’t sufficiently stabilize a displaced femoral-neck fracture in a young patient  
(C) Hip spica hinge brace – inadequate immobilization and fixation for this high-risk injury  
(D) Limited weight bearing with crutches – nonoperative management leads to high rates of nonunion/AVN  
(E) Operative reduction and internal fixation – standard of care in young patients with displaced femoral-neck fractures  

Step 4: Select the best option  
Operative (open) reduction with internal fixation restores anatomic alignment, stabilizes the fracture, and maximizes chances of union and femoral-head viability in a young adult.

Final Answer: \boxed{Operative\ reduction\ and\ internal\ fixation} (E)

– A normal (positive) NBT test turns blue when neutrophils generate superoxide (via NADPH oxidase)

Step 3: Correlate with disease.  
– Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) is due to NADPH oxidase deficiency  
– Patients with CGD are especially susceptible to catalase⁺ organisms, and their neutrophils cannot generate reactive oxygen species  

Step 4: Match to the answer choice.  
– The underlying defect in CGD is impaired phagocytic oxidative metabolism (failure of the respiratory burst to produce superoxide and downstream ROS)  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Impaired phagocytic oxidative metabolism}} (Choice H)

Step 1: Recall the steady-state principle for IV infusion  
At steady state, Rate in = Rate out  
Rate in = infusion rate (mg/h)  
Rate out = CL · Css  (where CL is clearance in L/h, Css is steady-state concentration in mg/L)

Step 2: Plug in the known values  
Infusion rate = 100 mg/h  
Css = 50 mg/L  

So  
100 mg/h = CL · (50 mg/L)

Step 3: Solve for CL  
CL = (100 mg/h) / (50 mg/L)  
CL = 2 L/h

Therefore, the clearance of Drug X in this man is  
\boxed{2\text{ L/h}}.

(B) Elbow flexion – C5–C6 (musculocutaneous nerve)

– He can still move his arms, so all functions supplied by roots *above* the level of injury are spared, but any functions supplied by roots *at or below* the injury will be lost.

– C5–C6 → elbow flexion is preserved.  
– C7 → elbow extension and wrist flexion are lost.

Step 1: Recognize the clinical syndrome  
– This patient, 2 days after fem‐pop bypass graft for claudication, now has acute worsening foot pain, pallor, a cool extremity, and absent distal pulses (weak popliteal, no pedal pulses).  
– He has the “6 Ps” of acute limb ischemia (pain, pallor, pulselessness, poikilothermia, paresthesias, paralysis).  

Step 2: Identify the most likely cause  
– In a postop patient with a newly placed graft, acute thrombosis/occlusion of the graft is most likely.  

Step 3: Review management priorities in acute limb ischemia  
1. Immediate anticoagulation with IV heparin to prevent propagation of the thrombus.  
2. Prompt revascularization to salvage the limb:  
   • Surgical thrombectomy/embolectomy is the gold standard when the lesion is surgically accessible (as in a bypass graft).  
   • Catheter‐directed thrombolysis (tPA) is reserved for patients in whom surgery is contraindicated or the occlusion is in a smaller distal vessel.  
3. Imaging:  
   • No time for MRA (too slow).  
   • Doppler ultrasound or CT angiography can be used if the diagnosis is unclear, but here the exam is straightforward.  
   • Surgical exploration with on‐table (intraoperative) angiography allows both confirmation of the lesion and immediate thrombectomy.  

Step 4: Eliminate incorrect answers  
(A) Compartment pressure measurements—used to diagnose compartment syndrome, but in compartment syndrome pulses are often preserved.  
(B) Doppler ultrasonography—would confirm occlusion but wastes time when the diagnosis is clear and limb viability is threatened.  
(C) Intra‐arterial tPA—slower, higher bleeding risk in a fresh surgical graft; not first‐line for an acutely thrombosed bypass graft.  
(E) MRA—too time‐consuming and not needed when clinical diagnosis is clear.  

Step 5: Select the best answer  
– Urgent surgical thrombectomy with on‐table angiography both confirms the site of occlusion and allows immediate restoration of blood flow.  

Final Answer: \boxed{D} Intraoperative angiography

Step 1: Identify the clinical scenario and decision point  
A 45-year-old woman with 6 months of amenorrhea, hot flashes, night sweats, and insomnia is seeking relief. The physician has already explained that hormone therapy can help and has discussed the associated risks.

Step 2: Recall principles of informed consent and shared decision-making  
Informed consent isn’t just about giving information; it also requires:  
1. Ensuring patient understanding of options, risks, and benefits  
2. Eliciting patient values, preferences, and concerns  
3. Guiding the patient toward a decision that fits her goals

Step 3: Evaluate each response option  
(A) “Could you tell me your thoughts about the hormone treatment option we have discussed?”  
    – Actively elicits the patient’s perspective and ensures her values drive the decision.  
(B) “I have many patients with similar symptoms, and everyone responds to hormone therapy differently.”  
    – A neutral statement but does not invite her input or check understanding.  
(C) “I will give you some written information to review, and then I can answer any remaining questions.”  
    – Useful for supplementing discussions but does not immediately engage her preferences.  
(D) “If your symptoms are really severe, the risk of hormone therapy may be worth it.”  
    – Imposes a judgment on the risk–benefit balance rather than exploring her view.

First, let’s outline the key acid–base effects of acute salicylate (aspirin) overdose:

1. Early (first few hours) effect: stimulation of respiratory center → primary respiratory alkalosis (↑ pH, ↓ PaCO₂).  
2. As salicylate levels rise: uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation → lactic acidosis and ketoacidosis → primary metabolic acidosis (↓ pH, ↓ HCO₃⁻).  
3. Thus by ~3 hours there is a mixed disorder:  
   – respiratory alkalosis (↓ PaCO₂)  
   – metabolic acidosis (↓ HCO₃⁻)  
   – net effect often is acidemia or near‐normal pH skewed slightly acid.  

We therefore predict:  
– pH: decreased (due to the metabolic acidosis component dominating)  
– PaCO₂: decreased (due to hyperventilation from respiratory alkalosis)  
– HCO₃⁻: decreased (due to metabolic acidosis)  

Looking at the options (arrows are for pH, PaCO₂, HCO₃⁻ respectively):

(A) ↑ ↓ ↑  
(B) ↓ ↓ ↓  
(C) ↑ ↑ ↓  
(D) ↓ ↓ ↑  
(E) ↑ ↑ ↑  

Only choice (B) matches ↓ pH, ↓ PaCO₂, ↓ HCO₃⁻.  

Final Answer: \boxed{B}

Step 1: Summarize key findings

– PFTs: ↓FEV₁/FVC (obstructive), ↑residual volume (air trapping), ↓DLCO  
– CXR: hyperinflation, hypertranslucent lower lobes  

Step 2: Interpret data  
– Obstructive pattern with air-trapping and ↓DLCO → emphysema rather than pure chronic bronchitis (which has normal DLCO) or asthma (reversible obstruction, normal/increased DLCO)  
– Lower-lobe predominance → panacinar emphysema, classically seen in α₁-antitrypsin deficiency but also fits the general diagnosis of emphysema  

Step 3: Rule out other choices  
A. Asthma – reversible airway obstruction, normal/increased DLCO  
B. Bronchiectasis – chronic sputum, but CT shows bronchial dilation; DLCO often normal  
C. Pulmonary fibrosis – restrictive PFTs (↑FEV₁/FVC), ↑DLCO or normal  
D. Cystic fibrosis – presents earlier in life with thick sputum, pancreatic problems, bronchiectasis  

Final Answer  
\boxed{E} Emphysema

Step 1: Determine whether the patient is “dead” by legal/medical criteria.  
  – Although he has a catastrophic infarction with herniation and is on maximal support, he is not yet declared brain‐dead (no clinical determination of irreversible cessation of all brain function has been made).  Organ procurement cannot legally begin until death is declared.  
  
Step 2: Check the patient’s documented wishes.  
  – His driver’s license indicates that he wishes to be an organ donor.  This is a legally binding expression of his intent (in most U.S. jurisdictions, a signed donor card or registry entry supersedes next‐of‐kin objections).  
  
Step 3: Involve the organ procurement organization (OPO).  
  – Federal regulations (the “Final Rule”) require hospitals to notify their local OPO of all deaths or imminent deaths.  Once notified, the OPO’s trained personnel should approach the family to discuss donation.  This preserves separation between clinical care and procurement discussions and ensures that families receive standardized, unbiased information.  
  
Step 4: Timing  
  – You do not “initiate organ donation” (i.e., procurement) until after death is declared (brain death or circulatory death, depending on protocol).  You also do not delay OPO notification simply because the family is upset; early involvement of the OPO is mandated once death is imminent or after death.  Arranging for the OPO to meet with the family now ensures that both the patient’s wishes and the family’s needs for information are handled appropriately.  
  
Therefore, the most appropriate next step is:  
\boxed{Arrange\ for\ the\ regional\ organ\ procurement\ organization\ to\ address\ the\ issue\ with\ the\ patient’s\ family.}

Step 1: Localize the lesion  
– A 5-day history of cranial nerve VI (abducens) and VII (facial) palsies plus ataxia localizes to the brainstem/cerebellar region.  

Step 2: Interpret the MRI  
– The classic MRI appearance (a T2-hyperintense, diffusely infiltrating lesion centered in the pons) is diagnostic of a diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (DIPG).  

Step 3: Prognosis and treatment options  
– DIPGs are essentially incurable with a median survival of under 1 year.  
– Surgical resection is not feasible due to the infiltrative nature and critical location.  
– Radiotherapy offers only temporary symptom relief.  
– There is no established chemotherapeutic regimen that significantly alters outcome.  
– Experimental trials exist, but none have proven curative.  

Step 4: Appropriate next step in management  
– Given the uniformly poor prognosis and lack of curative therapy, the immediate priority is to align care with the family’s values and goals.  

Final answer: \boxed{Discuss\ the\ family’s\ goals\ for\ the\ patient\ (C)}

Step 1: Recognize diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).  
– Glucose 850 mg/dL, HCO₃⁻ 6 mEq/L, deep (Kussmaul) respirations, polyuria/polydipsia, lethargy → DKA.  

Step 2: Initial priorities in DKA management  
1. Restore intravascular volume  
2. Correct electrolyte abnormalities (especially potassium)  
3. Start insulin  

Step 3: Fluid replacement  
– Begin with isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) to restore perfusion and dilute hyperglycemia.  

Step 4: Potassium management  
– Even if serum K⁺ is “normal” (4.1), total body K⁺ is depleted in DKA.  
– Do NOT start insulin therapy until there is adequate urine output and you know the patient is not hypokalemic.  
– Once urine output is confirmed, add K⁺ to IV fluids to prevent hypokalemia when insulin drives K⁺ intracellularly.  

Step 5: Insulin therapy  
– After volume repletion and K⁺ supplementation have begun, start a continuous insulin infusion.  

Step 6: Why the other choices are incorrect  
(B) Oral bicarbonate is generally reserved for pH <6.9; routine acidosis correction is via fluids and insulin.  
(C) Hypotonic saline would worsen hypernatremia risk; start with isotonic.  
(D) Phenobarbital is not indicated; hyponatremic seizures are prevented by careful fluid management.  
(E) Insulin can be started only after K⁺ is adequate; plus intermediate-acting alone is not appropriate initial therapy.  

Final answer: \boxed{Administer\ normal\ saline\ and\ add\ potassium\ once\ urinary\ output\ is\ adequate\ (A)}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical scenario  
– A woman of reproductive age with positive pregnancy test presents with vaginal spotting and lower quadrant pain. Ectopic pregnancy must be considered.  

Step 2: Review the imaging findings  
– Transvaginal ultrasound shows a thickened endometrial stripe but no intrauterine gestational sac and no adnexal mass. This is a nondiagnostic study in the evaluation of suspected early pregnancy.  

Step 3: Know the role of the β-hCG “discriminatory zone”  
– Transvaginal ultrasound reliably visualizes an intrauterine gestational sac once the quantitative serum β-hCG exceeds about 1,500–2,000 mIU/mL.  
– If the β-hCG is below that threshold, the pregnancy may simply be too early to see. If it is above the threshold and no sac is seen, ectopic pregnancy is highly likely.  

Step 4: Determine the next step  
– Because the ultrasound is nondiagnostic, the next step is to obtain quantitative serum β-hCG levels and repeat them in 48 hours:
   • A normal (viable) intrauterine pregnancy should show at least a 53% increase over 48 hours.  
   • A suboptimal rise (or plateau/fall) suggests ectopic pregnancy or failed intrauterine pregnancy (spontaneous abortion).  

Step 5: Select the answer  
– The most appropriate next step is serial serum β-hCG concentrations.  

Final Answer: \boxed{E}

– Diffuse vacuolar degeneration of basal epidermal cells plus a mononuclear (lymphocytic) infiltrate is characteristic of acute GVHD, in which donor T lymphocytes attack host epithelial cells.

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– The patient has a history of anterior MI and now presents with progressive fatigue, ankle swelling, JVD, crackles, an S3 gallop, and peripheral edema. These findings are classic for chronic systolic (heart failure with reduced ejection fraction) heart failure.

Step 2: Recall the hemodynamic and functional changes in systolic heart failure  
– In systolic dysfunction, the left ventricle has impaired contractility → ↓ ejection fraction → ↑ end-diastolic volume/pressure → ↑ pulmonary capillary wedge pressure → signs of pulmonary and systemic congestion.

Step 3: Analyze each answer choice  
(A) Decreased pulmonary capillary wedge pressure – wrong; it is increased in left-sided failure.  
(B) Impaired contractility of the left ventricle – correct; chronic post-MI remodeling leads to reduced LV contractile function.  
(C) Prolapse of the mitral valve – not a typical consequence of chronic systolic failure.  
(D) Thrombosis of the superior vena cava – would cause facial/upper-extremity swelling, not the findings here.  
(E) Ventricular septal defect – an acute post-MI VSD causes sudden hemodynamic collapse and a new murmur, not a 2-month gradual onset.

Final Answer: \boxed{B} Impaired contractility of the left ventricle.

Step 1: Identify the finding on exam and x-ray  
– Dullness to percussion and diminished breath sounds over the right lung base + the chest x-ray appearance indicate a pleural effusion.

Step 2: Classify the effusion in this clinical context  
– A patient with known lung adenocarcinoma most likely has a malignant pleural effusion, which is an exudative effusion.

Step 3: Recall Light’s criteria for exudates  
An exudative effusion meets at least one of the following (using pleural fluid compared to serum):  
  • Pleural fluid protein/serum protein > 0.5  
  • Pleural fluid LDH/serum LDH > 0.6  
  • Pleural fluid LDH more than two-thirds the upper limit of normal serum LDH  

Step 4: Predict the laboratory findings  
– Exudative effusions have a high protein content (i.e., increased total protein concentration).  
– Malignant effusions often also have low glucose (tumor cells consume glucose) and lymphocyte predominance, but the hallmark distinguishing exudates from transudates is the high protein.

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Increased total protein concentration (F)}}

Step 1: Identify the syndrome  
– The patient has central obesity, purple abdominal striae, hypertension, weight gain, and muscle wasting in the extremities.  
– Labs show markedly elevated cortisol (random and 24-h), ↑ACTH (at the upper limit of normal to high), with normal aldosterone and metanephrines.  
→ Diagnosis: ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome (most likely a pituitary adenoma).

Step 2: Recall cortisol’s effects on bone  
– Cortisol causes osteoporosis by:  
  • Decreasing osteoblast proliferation and function (↓ bone formation)  
  • Increasing osteoblast and osteocyte apoptosis  
  • Increasing osteoclast activity (↑ bone resorption)  
  • Reducing intestinal calcium absorption and increasing renal calcium excretion (further stimulating PTH and bone resorption)

Step 3: Link to the fracture  
– Osteoporosis in Cushing syndrome is primarily due to increased bone resorption by osteoclasts.

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
A. Calcium absorption – cortisol ↓ this  
B. Calcium excretion – cortisol ↑ this, but the main driver of osteoporosis is bone resorption  
C. Osteoblast proliferation – cortisol ↓ this  
D. Osteoclast proliferation – cortisol ↑ this → correct  
E. Phosphorus absorption – cortisol ↓ this  
F. Phosphorus excretion – cortisol ↑ this, but again secondary  

Final Answer: \boxed{Osteoclast\ proliferation\ (D)}

– The film (not shown here) demonstrates the humeral head displaced from the glenoid fossa in an anteroinferior direction  
– This is classic for an anterior glenohumeral dislocation

Step 3: Recall the mechanism of recurrent anterior dislocations  
– Initial dislocation often tears the anteroinferior joint capsule and labrum (a Bankart lesion)  
– This capsulolabral injury destabilizes the glenohumeral joint, predisposing to repeated dislocations

Step 4: Evaluate the answer choices

(C) Osteoarthritis of the glenohumeral joint – uncommon in a 20-year-old, and would show joint space narrowing, osteophytes  
(D) Rotator cuff detachment – patients can be passively moved, and findings are more related to weakness than gross instability  
(E) Weakness of the deltoid muscle – causes abduction weakness but not gross anterior instability or severe pain with passive motion

Final answer: \boxed{Damage\;to\;the\;capsular\;ligaments}

Step 1: Recognize acute pancreatitis  
– Severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, relieved by leaning forward  
– Elevated serum amylase  

Step 2: Identify etiology  
– Triglycerides are 1,500 mg/dL (normal < 150) → hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis (TG > 1,000 mg/dL is a well‐known risk)  

Step 3: Initial management of acute pancreatitis  
– IV fluids, analgesia, bowel rest (already started)  

Step 4: Specific therapy for hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis  
– Because his TG > 1,000 mg/dL and he has acute pancreatitis, rapid TG lowering is indicated  
– Therapeutic plasma exchange (apheresis) is the most effective way to acutely reduce TG levels in this setting  

Step 5: Long‐term management (once acute episode resolves)  
– Fibrate therapy (eg, gemfibrozil)  
– Lifestyle modifications (diet, weight loss, exercise)  

Most appropriate next step (acute phase): plasma apheresis.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Apheresis}}

(A) Airway compression – a large mediastinal mass can acutely compress the trachea/bronchi when the patient lies flat.  
(B) Cardiac arrhythmia – no history or ECG changes suggested.

– The acute worsening in the setting of a large anterior mediastinal mass and supine positioning is best explained by compression of the central airway.
